diagrams.title: Diamond Inheritance Without Virtual
diagrams.description: Shows inheritance hierarchy causing diamond problem - two separate copies of base class A exist in D
elements: Class B (inherits from A) on left
Class C (inherits from A) on right
Class D (inherits from B and C) at bottom
Two separate A boxes inside D
diagrams.purpose: Visualize the problem: duplicate base class members causing ambiguity in accessing A's members from D

diagrams.title: Diamond Inheritance With Virtual Base
diagrams.description: Same hierarchy but with virtual inheritance - only one shared copy of base class A exists in D
elements: Class D : public B, public C
Single shared A subobject in D
vbptr arrows pointing to shared A
diagrams.purpose: Visualize the solution: virtual inheritance ensures single instance of base class, eliminating diamond problem

diagrams.title: Constructor Call Order in Virtual Inheritance
diagrams.description: Shows the order in which constructors are executed when object of class D is created
elements: Step 2: Direct bases B and C constructors (left to right)
diagrams.purpose: Understand execution order: Virtual bases → Direct bases → Most-derived class, and that virtual base is called only once

diagrams.title: Memory Layout Comparison
diagrams.description: Object memory layout comparison between non-virtual and virtual inheritance
elements: Non-virtual: D contains B(A) + C(A) = two A subobjects
Virtual: D contains vbptr + B + C = one shared A
diagrams.purpose: Understand how virtual base pointer (vbptr) adds overhead but enables single base instance
